ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general roti flatbread entry; no specific tandoori roti entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tandoori roti code; A00LC (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code found for tandoori roti. General flatbread concept not mapped to a precise SNOMED CT term.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium tandoori roti weighs approximately 80 g and is typically served as an accompaniment to curries or dal.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Roti — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), Table: Cereal Products
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) reports tandoori roti made from whole wheat atta baked in a tandoor. Values used directly. Butter variant adds approximately 3–5 g fat per piece; per-100g values here represent the plain (no added butter) form as the base. Butter variant note included in notes field.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian flatbreads — Chapati and Roti (whole wheat, tandoor-baked); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for tandoori roti exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest analogous food is whole wheat chapati / roti baked at high heat, reported GI approximately 62 (medium) in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. Tandoori roti uses the same whole wheat atta base and similar baking conditions; GI 62 is a defensible analogous estimate. GL per serving = (62 × 42.8 g available carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 26.5, rounded to 26.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g); butter variant adds further sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid the butter variant, as added butter and salt can raise sodium intake; one plain tandoori roti (80 g) contributes approximately 168 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and high carbohydrate content (~53.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size; the medium GI of whole wheat tandoori roti produces a significant glycemic load per serving (~26.7), so pairing with high-fibre vegetables or legumes and limiting to one piece per meal is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat in plain form; whole wheat provides dietary fibre beneficial for lipid management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain tandoori roti is relatively low in saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) and provides 3.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; the butter variant should be avoided or minimised by individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori roti is a traditional North Indian whole wheat flatbread baked directly on the inner wall of a tandoor, a cylindrical clay oven heated to very high temperatures (typically 400–480 °C). The dough is made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and salt, rolled into a round disc and slapped onto the tandoor wall, where it bakes rapidly, developing characteristic charred spots and a slightly smoky flavour. The result is a semi-leavened flatbread with a crisp exterior and a soft, chewy interior. In the butter variant, melted butter or ghee is brushed over the surface immediately after baking, adding richness and a glossy finish. Tandoori roti is a staple accompaniment to North Indian curries, dal, and grilled meats, and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, high-carbohydrate food (approximately 264 kcal and 53.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with a meaningful dietary fibre contribution (3.8 g/100 g) from the whole wheat base. Its medium glycemic index (~62) means blood glucose rises moderately after consumption, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The plain form is low in saturated fat and suitable for most dietary patterns; the butter variant adds additional calories and saturated fat. Iron (2.7 mg/100 g) and B vitamins (thiamine, niacin) from the whole wheat flour contribute to micronutrient intake.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. GI estimated from the closest analogous food (whole wheat chapati/roti) in the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008, which is an approved GI source. All mandatory multilingual names for Indian cuisine served in Singapore are present.
notes: 1. Per-100g values represent the plain (no added butter) form. The butter variant adds approximately 3–5 g fat and 25–45 kcal per piece (80 g serving), depending on the amount of butter applied. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; naturally zero in plain whole wheat flatbread with no hydrogenated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plain roti contains no animal fat). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; no vitamin C in baked wheat flatbread). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients in plain form). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this item. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this item. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this item. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this item. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this item. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this item. 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati/roti from Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct tandoori roti GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. gi_category is medium (GI 56–69). 5. Dietary flags: is_vegan is false because the butter variant contains dairy; the plain form is vegan. is_dairy_free is false for the butter variant. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315571 is the closest parent roti flatbread entry; no specific tandoori roti term exists in FoodOn as of the accessed date.
